courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: German I
courses.description: As the first in a series of six language courses, this course invites students without prior knowledge of German to explore the basic features of the German language. Supported by audiovisual aids, students will receive intensive practice in spoken and written German through interactive class work, homework and online exercises. They will become familiar with the basic concepts of German grammar and learn how to interact in some simple everyday situations.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken GERM2000,2001,2050,3000,3001,3002,3050,3051 or 4001
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, the student should be able to deal with some simple daily situations in German such as greeting somebody and exchanging basic personal information with someone in a social gathering, talking about languages and professions, making assumptions, talking about preferences for food and drinks and going shopping.
courses.course_syllabus:
GERM1000

The course covers the first 3 units of the textbook “*Kontakte, German I and German II”*.

| Chapter | Topics | Culture | Structures |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Einführung A (Introduction A) | requests names clothes and colours greetings numbers | first names colours as symbols German way of counting German way of writing | A.1 Giving instructions: Polite commands A.2 What’s your name? The verb *heißen* A.3 The German case system A.4 Grammatical gender: Nouns and pronouns A.5 Addressing people: *Sie* versus *du* |
| Einführung B (Introduction B) | The classroom Descriptions Our body Our family Weather and seasons Nationality and origins | What is important in life? Weather and climate The location of Germany in Europe | B.1 Definite and indefinite articles B.2 Who are you? The verb *sein* B.3 What do you have? The verb *haben* B.4 Plural forms of nouns B.5 Personal pronouns B.6 Origins: Woher kommen Sie? B.7 Possessive adjectives: *mein* and *dein/Ihr* |
| Chapter 1 | Spare time School and university Daily routine Personal information | Leisure time German school system | 1.1 The present tense 1.2 Expressing likes and dislikes : gern/nicht gern 1.3 Telling Time 1.4 Word order in statements 1.5 Separable préfix verbs 1.6 Word order in questions |
courses.feedback_evaluation: Our programme highly values students’ feedback and comments and is happy to use them for reflection on our teaching and improvement. Students are very welcome to provide comments and feedback on the course any time to their course teacher or the course level coordinator through email or in personal conversation. In addition, students’ feedbacks will be collected in the middle of the term through an open-end questionnaire and the teacher will discuss the feedbacks in class and make improvements if necessary. The course will also follow the university’s course evaluation exercise at the end of the term, and students’ feedbacks will be used for future course planning and teaching.
courses.required_readings:
Textbook: (available at The Commercial Press University Bookstore)

• Tschirner, Nikolai, Terrell (2012) Kontakte, German I & German II, Singapore: McGraw-Hill.

Readings & references:

• Reimann, Monika (2001) Essential grammar of German with exercises. Ismaning: Hueber

• Dodd, Bill et al. (1996) Modern German grammar: a practical guide. London: Routledge

• A desk-size bilingual dictionary, such as Langenscheidt, Collins or Duden-Oxford (pocket-size dictionaries are not recommended).

Web-based learning support:

• Beolingus online dictionary (English-German: http://dict.tu-chemnitz.de/)

• Leo online dictionary (English-Chinese, English German www.leo.org)

• Please refer to the Blackboard sites for GERM1000 and GERM0000 for online exercises and further materials

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: First Step in German
courses.description:
[This course is offered via asynchronous online mode. Students should register the course via KEEP, a CUHK eLearning platform where the course materials are uploaded.]

This online self-learning course invites students without prior knowledge of German to explore some basic features of the German language and culture through a virtual journey. Based on texts, audio and video input, and interactive tasks, students will be able to practice both spoken and written German. This course serves as a taster course for students who may consider taking German courses at CUHK in the future.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
Not for students who have taken GERM1000,2000,2001,2050,3000,3001,3002,3050,3051or4001
From 2023-24, students can only earn up to 2 units of these courses: CLCP1001,CLCP1101,CLCC1001,CLCC1101,JASP1350,HKSL1001,SPAN1001,GERM1001,KORE1001,FREN1001
courses.course_syllabus:
| | **Course Content** | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Sections** | **Speech acts/Topics** | **Grammar** | **Culture** |
| **Unit 1** Introducing yourself | * Greeting people * Introducing oneself * Giving and asking basic personal information * The German alphabet * Pronouncing and spelling simple words | * Formal and informal form of address; personal pronouns * selected verbs and their conjugation: sein, kommen, heißen * interrogative pronouns, and question | * Greetings and their regional differences: Guten Tag, Auf Wiedersehen, Hallo, Tschüs, Grüezi, Ade.... * German names and surnames * Formal and informal use of language * German-speaking countries: die Schweiz, Österreich, Deutschland |
| **Unit 2** Languages and Countries | * Talking about countries and languages * Talking about other people * Saying “Yes” and “No” | * The 3rd person singular of the present tense * The verb “to speak” * Yes (*“ja”*) and No (*“nein”*) in the sentence * numbers 0-9 | * Geographical names/languages: Deutschland, Deutsch, etc. * International words: Taxi, Information, Polizei, Restaurant, Bar, Telefon etc. |
| **Unit 3** Food! | * Talking about food * Expressing likes/dislikes * Understanding prices * Ordering at restaurants | * The verbs “*mögen*”, “*trinken*”, “*essen*” in singular * negation with “*nicht*” * The verb “*nehmen*” in singular * The usage of “*bitte*”, “*danke*” | * Typical dishes of German-speaking countries * Information about and names of selected dishes, and their regional prevalence * German restaurant etiquette and ordering |
| **Unit 4** Spare-time activities | * Discussing hobbies * Expressing preference for various spare time activities | * Verb conjugations in the plural * Sentences with “*gerne*” * The verb “*finden*” | * German football: Culture, clubs, players etc. * Other popular sports: Biathlon, skiing, tennis,... |
courses.feedback_evaluation: An online course evaluation exercise will be conducted at the end of the course, and students’ feedbacks will be used for future course planning and teaching.

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: German II

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404

courses.description: Building on the knowledge acquired in GERM1000, this course aims to further develop students’ ability to communicate in German in various everyday situations. It also seeks to give students a taste of German culture and invites them to explore cultural differences. Through intensive practice students will develop more confidence in interacting in German while further expanding and solidifying their understanding of the basic grammar and pronunciation patterns.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1. Not for students who have taken GERM2001,2050,3000,3001,3002,3050,3051 or 4001
2. Prerequisite: GERM1000
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, the student should be able to deal with some simple daily situations in German such as
- Shopping in a supermarket
- Talking about family and relatives
- Discussing daily routines and leisure activities
- Giving/receiving directions and telling time
courses.course_syllabus:
| Course Content: the course will cover units 4 to 7 of Tangram Aktuell 1 (vol. 1 and 2) | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 4 Im Supermarkt In the supermarket | - Shopping in the supermarket - Finding your way around the supermarket - Asking politely for advice and assistance | - Dative case of personal pronouns and locations - Imperative - Food, measurements and packaging |
| 5 Arbeit und Freizeit Work and leisure | - Discussing advantages and disadvantages - Leisure activities - Telling time and making appointments - Dates - Expressing frequency | - Modal verbs - Locations and directions - Expressing time - Ordinal numbers |
| 6 Familie und Haushalt Family and household | - Family and relatives - Discussing household chores - Daily routines | - Possesive articles - Separable prefix verbs - two-way prepositions - conjunctions (*und, aber, oder*) |
| 7 Berlin! Berlin! | - Giving/receiving directions - Speaking about past events - Discussing favorite places | - Locations and directions - The present perfect tense - Past perfect participle - Personal pronouns in the accusative case |

In addition, students’ feedbacks will be collected in the middle of the term through an open-end questionnaire and the teacher will discuss the feedbacks in class and make improvements if necessary. The course will also follow the university’s course evaluation exercise at the end of the term, and students’ feedbacks will be used for future course planning and teaching.

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: German III
courses.description: This course aims to further enhance students’ communication skills in the four language domains listening, speaking, reading and writing. Students are expected to communicate confidently in a wider range of social interactions including more formal situations and simple professional contexts as well as reporting past events and sharing experience. At the end of this course, students will have completed a proficiency level comparable to level A1 of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages (CEFR).
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1. Not for students who have taken GERM3000,3001,3002,3050, 3051 or 4001
2. Prerequisite: GERM2000 or 2050
courses.course_syllabus:
| Course Content: The course will cover 4 units of the textbook Studio D A1. | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Lesson | Speech acts | Grammar |
| Station 2 | Revision of Einheit 5-8 | |
| 9. Ferien und Urlaub (Holiday and vacation) | - Talk about holidays and vacation in different European countries - Describe an accident - Describe one’s own vacation | - Perfect tense of regular and irregular verbs |
| 10. Essen und Trinken (Food and drinks) | - Shopping: handle conversations in a shop, such as request for items, ask for prices - Talk about food, likes and dislikes - Understand and explain a recipe | - Frequency: jeden Tag, manchmal, nie - Question Word: welch- - Comparatives: viel, gut, gern |
| 11. Kleidung und Wetter (Clothing and weather) | - Talk about clothes - Buy clothes - Give information on colours and sizes - Understand weather information - Talk about the weather | - Adjectives in Akkusativ – indefinite articles - Demonstrative pronouns: dieser – dieses – diese/ der – das - die - Personal pronoun: es |
| 12. Körper und Gesundheit (Body and health) | - Name body parts - Express health problems and where it hurts - Give recommendations and instructions - Write a letter | - Imperatives - Modal Verb dürfen - Personal pronouns in Akkusativ |

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: Sounds of German
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the phonetic system of the German language, with an emphasis on the similarities and differences between German and English pronunciation. Illustrations and examples will be taken from modern spoken German. Students learning German are encouraged to take this course.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- To learn about different forms of Sounds in the German language

e.g. dialogues, prose, poems, rhymes, songs etc.

- to get acquainted with the German phonetic structure

- to improve pronunciation and intonation

- to speak better German

- to have fun with the German language
courses.course_syllabus:
First contact + Adjustment of standard

The 5 vowels (long and short)

The long vowel of O and E and the endings

The Umlauts : Ä, Ö and Ü and

the Diphthongs au / eu / ei

The sounds of “ch” and “sch”

The sounds of “R”

Holiday (Chung Yeung Festival)

The sounds of the numbers

Mid Term Test

The melody

The accent and the rhythm

Combination and changes

The tongue-twisters

Final Test

course teacher or the course level coordinator through email or in personal conversation. In addition, students’ feedbacks

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: Exploring German Literature
courses.description:
This course is designed to introduce students to some most representative figures in German literature. Emphasis is placed on the critical and analytical reading of their major works in English or Chinese version.
All readings and discussions are in English. No knowledge of German is required.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course students demonstrate the ability to

• classify the important periods, writers and works of German literature.

• understand and analyze literary works as reflections of the social and political contexts of their time.

• distinguish various literary forms, such as drama, novel, poetry and short stories.
courses.course_syllabus:
GERM2100

| Topic | Contents / fundamental concepts |
| --- | --- |
| Periods of German Literature | Overview over the development from the beginnings to present |
| The beginnings | “Song of the Nibelungs” (*Das Nibelungenlied*) - ~1200 - loyalty and betrayal as the fundamental concepts in medieval epics |
| Literature in the period of Enlightenment (*Aufklärung)* - | Lessing (1729-1781), “Nathan the Wise” (*Nathan der Weise)* – theater to promote religious tolerance |
| Storm and Stress (*Sturm und Drang*) | The young Goethe (1749-1832): “The Sorrows of Young Werther” (Die Leiden des jungen Werthers) –passionate individualism |
| Weimar Classic: Goethe and Schiller | The birth of a national German literature – breaking the barriers of human existence (Goethe “*Faust*”) and struggle against oppression (Schiller “Intrigues and Love” - *Kabale und Liebe*). |
| Romanticism | Kleist (1797-1856): “The Beggar-woman of Locarno” (*Das Bettelweib von Locarno*) – Eichendorff (1788-1857): “Moon Night” (*Mondnacht*) - the irrational and the mystic |
| Young Germany | The making of modern social drama: Büchner (1813-1837): *Woyzeck* |
| Expressionism | Kafka (1883-1924), “Metamorphosis” (*Die Verwandlung*) |
| Swiss-German Literature | Dürrenmatt (1921-1990): “The Visit of the old Lady” (*Der Besuch der alten Dame*) |
| Post-unification German Literature | Dealing with the past (fascism and neonazism) – Grass (1927- ) “Crabwalk” (*Im Krebsgang*) |
| Poetry after 1945 | Literature and the Holocaust: Paul Celan (1920-1970): “Death fugue” (*Todesfuge*) |

Kim, Vivian (1992) A concise history of German literature to 1900. Columbia: Camden House.

Stoehr, Ingo Roland (2001) German literature of the twentieth century: from aestheticism to postmodernism. Rochester, N.Y.: Camden House.

 All books are put on reserve in the University Library.

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: German Cinema
courses.description:
German cinema has always captured the attention of the world. This course is an introduction to the history and major developments in German cinema. It explores the language of cinema and the aesthetic strategies used by filmmakers. Through analyses and discussions about the making and content of the films,
students will not only get to know the films as entertaining art, but embed them in a social and historical context. Films are screened outside class hours. All films are with English subtitles, all readings and discussions in English. No knowledge of German is required.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students can:
• analyze key works of German cinema since the 1990s
• explore the relationship between film and culture/history
• talk about differences and similarities between film and one’s own perception of culture
• analyze several aesthetic and cultural concepts related to film
• analyze film scenes using tools learned during the course
courses.course_syllabus:
| **Week** | **Movie** | **Film language** | **Culture/ History** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Week 1 | | Film language: Introduction Film posters Trailer | German Cinema: Introduction |
| Week 2 | **Run Lola Run** Original: Lola rennt Year: 1998 Director: Tom Tykwer | Genre Opening Credits Symbols and motifs | A thriller film following several runs of a woman (Lola). Short, innovative, and highly unique. |
| Week 3 | **Run Lola Run** | Characters Sound Camera | contemporary urban life |
| Week 4 | **Run Lola Run** | Camera | free will vs determinism |
| Week 5 | **Good Bye Lenin**! Year: 2003 Director: Wolfgang Becker | Lighting | A comedy film about a boy who keeps the life of his mother firmly rooted in a world that no longer exists for the sake of her health. A lighter film set in the rapidly changing world. Fall of the Wall (East and West Germany) German reunification |
| Week 6 | **Good Bye Lenin**! | *Mise-en-scene* | Values (loss of GDR culture) Attitude towards life |
| Week 7 | **The Edukators** Original: Die fetten Jahre sind vorbei Year: 2004 Director: Hans Weingartner | Genre Set & Costumes | ``` ``` ``` The film is about the unconventional methods, opportunities and difficulties of young adults to engage in social injustice. ``` ``` ``` social injustice (methods, possibilities, difficulties) |
| Week 8 | **The Edukators** | Camera Sound | The 1968 generation (today’s social status, ideational legacy) forms of protest |
| Week 9 | **The Lives of Others** Original: Das Leben der anderen Year: 2006 Director: Florian Henckel von Donnersmarck | Sound | “Lives of Others” is one of the most powerful films depicting the dystopian invasion of personal privacy and political murder committed by the East German regime. The GDR regime Stasi: The secret police of GDR |
| Week 10 | **The Lives of Others** | Directors and acting *Mise-en-scene* | “Lives of Others” is one of the most powerful films depicting the dystopian invasion of personal privacy and political murder committed by the East German regime. surveillance and privacy protection |
| Week 11 | **Downfall** Original: Der Untergang Year: 2004 Director: Oliver Hirschbiegel | Script | Downfall” is a film depicting the last days of the Nazi party. It is a critically acclaimed movie that gives a personal look into the face of evil. Nazi Germany |
| Week 12 | **Downfall** | *Mise-en-scene* | Right-wing extremism |
| Week 13 | | | Summary and discussions |

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: German IV
courses.description: This course aims at increasing students' communicative fluency in oral and written German. Using a variety of classroom activities and multimedia, students will build up their competence in the four language domains listening, speaking, reading and writing. Through intensive practice they will be able to communicate in a wide range of social situations with the appropriate awareness of the pragmatic rules involved. Students receive guidance in the process of composing longer texts displaying a range of registers and styles relating to past, present and future events.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1. Not for students who have taken GERM3001,3002,3050,3051 or 4001
2. Prerequisite: GERM2001
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, the student should be able to:
- Talk about his or her learning biography
- Talk about family and related issues
- Plan or book a trip in German
- Speak about hobbies.
courses.course_syllabus:
| Course Content: The course will cover 4 units of the textbook Studio D A2. | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Lesson | Speech acts | Grammar |
| 1. Sprachen und Biografien | - Talking about languages - Giving reasons - Talking about one’s own learning biography | - Subordinate clause with ‘weil’ - Comparatives - Comparison of ‘wie’ and ‘als’ - Superlatives: ‘am höchsten’, ‘am weitesten’ |
| 2. Familienalbum | - Talking about family and family celebrations - Describing photos and persons - Congratulating someone, inviting someone - Expressing views | - Possessive articles in ‘Dativ’ - Adjectives in ‘Dativ’ - Subordinate clauses with ‘dass’ - Genitive |
| 3. Reisen und Mobilität | - Talking about a journey - Expressing assumptions: *wahrscheinlich/ vielleicht* - Reading travel itineraries - Planning and booking a trip - Expressing contraries: *aber* - Expressing alternatives: *oder* | Modal verb: *sollen* |
| 4. Aktiv in der Freizeit | - Talking about leisure time activities - Speaking about hobbies and interests - Reacting positively or negatively | - Reflexive pronouns - Verbs with prepositions - Indefinite words |
courses.feedback_evaluation: Our programme highly values students’ feedback and comments and is happy to use them for reflection on our teaching and improvement. Students are very welcome to provide comments and feedback on the course any time to their course teacher or the course level coordinator through email or in personal conversation. In addition, students’ feedbacks will be collected in the middle of the term through an open-end questionnaire and the teacher will discuss the feedbacks in class and make improvements if necessary. The course will also follow the university’s course evaluation exercise at the end of the term, and students’ feedbacks will be used for future course planning and teaching.

Readings & references:
• Reimann, Monika (2001) Essential grammar of German with exercises. Ismaning: Hueber
• Dodd, Bill et al. (1996) Modern German grammar: a practical guide. London: Routledge
• A desk-size bilingual dictionary, such as Langenscheidt, Collins or Duden-Oxford (pocket-size dictionaries are not recommended).
Web-based learning support:
• Online homework can be found on Blackboard https://elearn.cuhk.edu.hk
• Beolingus online dictionary (English-German: http://dict.tu-chemnitz.de/)
• Leo online dictionary (German/English /Chinese www.leo.org )
• Please refer to the Blackboard sites GERM2001 and GERM0000 for online exercises and further materials

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: German V
courses.description: This course focuses on the further development of communication skills in German with emphasis on the expression of personal views and the training of argumentative strategies. The course will also engage students in critical reflections on various aspects of life in German-speaking countries and their own social environment. Students will be guided to analyze various types of data to support their views. They will learn how to express their wishes and aspirations using the appropriate registers.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1. Not for students who have taken GERM3002,3051 or 4001
2. Prerequisite: GERM3000 or GERM3050
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, the student should be able to:
- describe locations while reporting about features of nature, geography and scenery in Germany.
- discuss causes and consequences from various perspectives in discussions of family relationships.
- report about events of the past, both in discussion of individual experience and in (re-)narrating stories.
- identify basic generic elements of a fairy tale comparing/contrasting these elements across versions.
courses.course_syllabus:
| Course Content: The course will cover the units 5 - 8 of the textbook Studio D A2. | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Lesson | Speech acts | Grammar |
| Unit 5: “Medien” Media | - talking about media - writing personal messages (sms, email) - making a complaint (about quality) | - indirect questions in subordinate sentences: if-sentences, indirect questions with interrogatives - adjectives without article -indefinite pronouns: „niemand, wenige, viele, alle“ |
| Unit 6: „Ausgehen“ Going out | - leisure time activities – expressing preferences - reading a restaurant menu - ordering in a restaurant - talking about dating and contacts | - Personal pronouns in Dativ („mit dir, mit ihm“) - Relative clause, relative pronouns in Nominativ and Akkusativ |
| Unit 7: “Zu Hause” At home | - talking about living in the city and in the countryside - analyzing property advertisements - looking for an apartment - planning to move house - reporting accidents in the household | - modal verbs in simple past - subordinate clauses with “als” |
| Unit 8: “Kultur erleben” Experience culture | - talking about cultural interests - planning a city tour - going to the theatre - talking and writing about events in the past | - temporal adverbs “damals, früher / heute,jetzt“ - verbs in simple past - perfect tense and simple past |
courses.feedback_evaluation: Our programme highly values students’ feedback and comments and is happy to use them for reflection on our teaching and improvement. Students are very welcome to provide comments and feedback on the course any time to their course teacher or the course level coordinator through email or in personal conversation. In addition, students’ feedbacks will be collected in the middle of the term through an open-end questionnaire and the teacher will discuss the feedbacks in class and make improvements if necessary. The course will also follow the university’s course evaluation exercise at the end of the term, and students’ feedbacks will be used for future course planning and teaching.
courses.required_readings:
Textbooks:
• Reader with texts will be handed out in class
• Link, Caroline (1996) Jenseits der Stille (DVD) (available at Amazon and in the CUHK library). Purchase of this material is mandatory.
courses.recommended_readings:
Readings &references:
• Dodd, Bill et al. (1996) Modern German grammar: a practical guide. London: Routledge
• Reimann, Monika (2001) Essential grammar of German with exercises. Ismaning: Hueber Available in the Swindon book store on campus)
• Beolingus online dictionary (English-German: http://dict.tu-chemnitz.de/)
• A desk-size bilingual dictionary, such as Langenscheidt, Collins or Duden-Oxford (pocket-size dictionaries are not recommended.
Web-based learning support:
• Additional material will be available on the course website (Blackboard)

courses.subject: GERM
courses.title: German VI
courses.description: This course builds on the knowledge acquired through previous German language courses. It aims at leading students to a proficiency level comparable to the threshold from A2 to B1 of the Common European Framework for Languages (CEFR), becoming “independent users” of German. To achieve this goal, students will be encouraged to work project-based through independent or collaborative work with their peers. At the end of this course students will have mastered the most important grammar features of German and be able to communicate effectively in a wide range of everyday situations and a number of professional contexts. They will be able to use their oral and written language skills to relate to, reflect on and analyze past and current issues involving the German-speaking societies.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
1. Not for students who have taken GERM3051 or 4001
2. Prerequisite: GERM3001
courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, participants will be able to use German according to the level A2 of the Common European Framework. This includes written and oral interaction.
courses.course_syllabus:
| Course Content: The course will cover units 9 to 12 of Studio D vol. 2. | | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Lesson | Speech acts | Grammar |
| 9 Arbeitswelten Work environments | - talking about career paths and career options - analyse job advertisements and CVs - telephone messages - polite requests | - connecting sentences with “denn” and “weil” - the verb “werden” (to become) - word formation: verb to noun - wishes, politeness “hätte“, „könnte“ |
| 10 Feste und Geschenke Festivals and presents | - talking about festivals and customs - talking about presents - compare festivals in German-speaking countries and other countries | - prepositions with Dativ - verbs with Dativ objects - verbs with Dativ and Akkusativ objects |
| 11 Mit allen Sinnen With all senses | - recognizing emotions - expressing emotions - reacting on emotions - talking about a movie | - indefinite pronouns “einige, manche” - verbs with prepositions in Akkusativ or Dativ (“liegen – legen”) - understanding Genitiv - relative clauses: „in, mit“ + Dativ |
| 12 Erfindungen und Erfinder Inventions and inventors | - understanding the manuals of products and inventions - working with texts - talking about purpose - describing processes | - subordinate clauses with “um … zu” and “damit” - passive voice (das Passiv) with „werden / wurden“ |
courses.feedback_evaluation: Our programme highly values students’ feedback and comments and is happy to use them for reflection on our teaching and improvement. Students are very welcome to provide comments and feedback on the course any time to their course teacher or the course level coordinator through email or in personal conversation. In addition, students’ feedbacks will be collected in the middle of the term through an open-end questionnaire and the teacher will discuss the feedbacks in class and make improvements if necessary. The course will also follow the university’s course evaluation exercise at the end of the term, and students’ feedbacks will be used for future course planning and teaching.
